findings.title: ActiveWindowInfo uses pid 0 as sentinel, conflicting with real PID 0
findings.description: Encapsulation: 7/10, Expression: 4/10, Usefulness: 5/10, Enforcement: 3/10 (Aggregate: 4.75). parseAppleScriptOutput returns { appName: '', windowTitle: '', pid: 0 } when there is no output, and also returns pid 0 when parsing fails (line 34). PID 0 is a valid Unix process (kernel/init). Callers cannot distinguish 'no active window detected' from 'the kernel process is frontmost'. The empty-string + zero sentinel pattern makes illegal states representable: a return value that looks valid but means 'no data'. This violates Make Illegal States Unrepresentable.
findings.suggestion: Return null from parseAppleScriptOutput when no data is available, or use a discriminated union result type: { ok: true, info: ActiveWindowInfo } | { ok: false, reason: string }. Alternatively, make pid optional (pid?: number) so callers must handle the absence case explicitly.

findings.title: ShortcutManagerOptions.unregister is accepted but never consumed
findings.description: Encapsulation: 7/10, Expression: 5/10, Usefulness: 3/10, Enforcement: 5/10 (Aggregate: 5.0). ShortcutManagerOptions requires callers to provide an 'unregister' function, but ShortcutManager's constructor (global-shortcuts.ts:18-24) never stores or uses it. Only 'unregisterAll' is stored. This is a type contract violation: the interface promises the manager will use per-shortcut unregistration, but it only performs bulk unregister. Callers are forced to implement a function that is dead code.
findings.suggestion: Either remove 'unregister' from ShortcutManagerOptions and only keep 'unregisterAll', or store and use it in ShortcutManager (e.g., for individual shortcut removal). If individual removal is planned for the future, add a comment and make it optional: unregister?: (accelerator: string) => void.

findings.title: ClipboardMonitor accepts zero or negative pollIntervalMs without validation
findings.description: Encapsulation: 8/10, Expression: 5/10, Usefulness: 5/10, Enforcement: 3/10 (Aggregate: 5.25). ClipboardMonitorOptions.pollIntervalMs is typed as optional number with a sensible default (1000ms). However, there is no validation that the provided value is positive. A caller passing pollIntervalMs: 0 or pollIntervalMs: -100 would create a setInterval with undefined behavior (Node.js clamps to 1ms, but the intent is clearly wrong). Constructor does not establish the invariant that pollIntervalMs > 0.
findings.suggestion: Add a guard in the constructor: if (this.pollIntervalMs <= 0) throw new Error('pollIntervalMs must be positive'). Alternatively, use Math.max(1, ...) as a floor. For stronger type-level enforcement, consider a branded PositiveNumber type, though that may be excessive for this context.

findings.title: ClipboardChange and ActiveWindowInfo are mutable event/result interfaces
findings.description: Encapsulation: 5/10, Expression: 5/10, Usefulness: 5/10, Enforcement: 5/10 (Aggregate: 5.0). ActiveWindowInfo represents a snapshot of the active window at a point in time -- it should be immutable once created. ClipboardChange is an event emitted by ClipboardMonitor -- events should be immutable as they may be consumed by multiple handlers. Neither interface uses readonly modifiers. This is a consistent pattern across the codebase (context-engine, persona-engine DTOs also lack readonly), but desktop-shell events are particularly important to protect since they cross module boundaries.
findings.suggestion: Add readonly to all fields on both interfaces: export interface ActiveWindowInfo { readonly appName: string; readonly windowTitle: string; readonly pid: number } and similarly for ClipboardChange. This prevents accidental mutation by consumers without any runtime cost.

findings.title: ClipboardChange.timestamp and pollIntervalMs use bare number for ms values
findings.description: Encapsulation: 7/10, Expression: 5/10, Usefulness: 5/10, Enforcement: 5/10 (Aggregate: 5.5). ClipboardChange.timestamp (line 11) and ClipboardMonitorOptions.pollIntervalMs (line 15) both use bare 'number' for millisecond values. While pollIntervalMs has a clear 'Ms' suffix convention, timestamp does not indicate its unit. This is consistent with the codebase-wide pattern of primitive obsession for timestamps/durations (flagged in previous reviews for AnimaOrchestrator, cron-service, evening-pipeline). A branded Milliseconds type or at minimum a JSDoc @unit annotation would clarify intent.
findings.suggestion: At minimum, add JSDoc: /** Unix epoch milliseconds */ timestamp: number. For stronger enforcement, consider a shared branded type: type EpochMs = number & { readonly __brand: 'EpochMs' }. This is a low-priority codebase-wide pattern.

positive_observations: ShortcutAction is a proper 4-value literal union ('toggle-panel' | 'voice-input' | 'quick-chat' | 'show-log') -- consistent with codebase best practices for domain enumerations.
ClipboardMonitor and ShortcutManager both use private readonly fields with options-object constructors -- good encapsulation pattern consistent with the codebase (SmartTip, MemoryExtractor, etc.).
Dependency injection via options interfaces (ClipboardMonitorOptions, ShortcutManagerOptions) keeps Imperative Shell concerns testable without requiring mocks of system APIs.
ClipboardMonitor.poll() properly wraps errors with context (new Error('ClipboardMonitor poll failed', { cause: err })) and delegates to onError callback -- good error handling pattern.
parseAppleScriptOutput is extracted as a pure function separate from the I/O in getActiveWindow -- clean Functional Core / Imperative Shell separation.
